critic_raw:
The primary metric for evaluation is Recall on malignant lesions, and model selection on the validation set is also guided by Recall. When you optimize and select models purely by maximizing recall, you can trivially achieve near-perfect recall by classifying everything as malignant — the experiment provides no lower bound on precision or specificity during selection, meaning the winning model could simply be the one that is most aggressive at predicting positive, not the most discriminating one.

The stratified random split preserves class proportions but ignores patient-level clustering. HAM10000 and ISIC datasets contain multiple images from the same patient. A random split will scatter images from the same patient across train and test, allowing the model to effectively memorize patient-specific skin texture, hair follicle patterns, and imaging artifacts rather than learning generalizable lesion features. This is a form of identity leakage that inflates test performance.

The experiment reports AUROC as a secondary metric but makes accuracy claims against traditional methods using recall as primary. Without reporting at a matched operating point, the comparison between CNN and color histogram baseline is not apples-to-apples — the baseline may achieve the same recall at lower precision, or the CNN's recall advantage could disappear at the threshold the baseline uses.

The dataset spans a decade of imaging conditions, but the design does not account for scanner drift or protocol changes over time. Normalization fitted on training data addresses intensity scale but does not correct for systematic shifts in imaging equipment or annotation practices that changed over a decade. If training data is predominantly from older scanners and test data from newer ones, generalization estimates will be biased.
all_issues_raised: Optimizing model selection exclusively on Recall without a precision floor trivially rewards aggressive positive classifiers rather than discriminating ones, making the winning model selection criterion uninformative.

HAM10000 and ISIC contain multiple images per patient; a stratified random split at the image level — not patient level — allows patient identity leakage across train and test, inflating estimated test performance.

The CNN vs. baseline comparison uses recall as primary metric without a matched operating point, making the comparison between methods non-equivalent when the two models operate at different threshold calibrations.

Normalization fitted on training data does not correct for decade-spanning scanner drift or protocol changes in annotation practice, biasing generalization estimates if imaging equipment distribution differs between splits.
all_issues_adjudicated: Optimizing model selection exclusively on Recall without a precision floor trivially rewards aggressive positive classifiers rather than discriminating ones, making the winning model selection criterion uninformative.

HAM10000 and ISIC contain multiple images per patient; a stratified random split at the image level — not patient level — allows patient identity leakage across train and test, inflating estimated test performance.

The CNN vs. baseline comparison uses recall as primary metric without a matched operating point, making the comparison between methods non-equivalent when the two models operate at different threshold calibrations.

Normalization fitted on training data does not correct for decade-spanning scanner drift or protocol changes in annotation practice, biasing generalization estimates if imaging equipment distribution differs between splits.
